required.title: [Read Aloud] The Story of Ruby Bridges
required.author: Robert Coles

required.title: [Read Aloud] Martin's Big Words: The Life of Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr.
required.author: Doreen Rappaport

suggested.title: [Read Aloud] The Smallest Girl in the Smallest Grade
suggested.author: Justin Roberts

suggested.title: [Read Aloud] Brave Girl: Clara and the Shirtwaist Makers' Strike of 1909
suggested.author: Michelle Markel

competence_markers.description: The student can distinguish between tattling and standing up and explain the test that separates them
The student can narrate the story of Ruby Bridges with specific detail and personal reflection
The student can explain King's approach to fighting injustice and articulate why he chose nonviolence
The student understands that helping the community is a responsibility, not optional charity
The student can articulate why staying silent when something wrong is happening is itself a choice with consequences
The student can express disagreement respectfully and understands why this is more effective than disrespectful disagreement
The student has completed a community contribution and can narrate what they learned from it
The student demonstrates developing moral courage in everyday situations — speaking up, being honest, helping others

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me the story of Ruby Bridges.
narration_prompts.detail: How old was she? What did she face? Where did her courage come from?

narration_prompts.prompt: What is the difference between tattling and standing up?
narration_prompts.detail: Give me an example of each. How do you tell the difference?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about Martin Luther King Jr. and how he fought injustice.
narration_prompts.detail: Why did he refuse to use violence? Do you think his approach worked?

narration_prompts.prompt: Have you ever seen something wrong happening and not said anything?
narration_prompts.detail: What happened? How did it feel? What would you do differently now?

narration_prompts.prompt: Why is helping your community a responsibility and not just a nice thing to do?
narration_prompts.detail: What does the community give you? What do you owe in return?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about a time when being honest was hard.
narration_prompts.detail: What made it hard? What happened? What did you learn?

cross_connections.description: The character formation in IC1.2 provides the foundation for the moral courage developed here. Integrity — matching your actions to your beliefs — is what makes standing up possible.

Understanding why rules exist gives the student a framework for evaluating when rules are just and when they are not. You cannot meaningfully resist unjust rules without understanding what makes rules legitimate.
Fairness as a concept, developed in 1.4.3, is the foundation for recognizing unfairness and knowing when to speak up.
The ethical reasoning developed in 1.4.4 — right versus wrong in the hard cases — provides the intellectual tools for the moral decisions studied here.
Standing up takes emotional strength. The resilience developed in IC1.3 supports the capacity to act under social pressure.
The courage journal and narration exercises develop writing skills alongside moral reflection. Written reflection deepens moral reasoning.

content.why_this_matters:
You already know what it feels like when something is unfair. Maybe someone cut in front of you in line. Maybe a bigger kid took something from a smaller kid. Maybe an adult made a rule that did not make any sense to you. You knew it was wrong — you felt it in your stomach before you could explain it with words.

But knowing something is wrong and doing something about it are not the same thing. It takes courage to speak up. It takes even more courage to speak up when the person doing the wrong thing is bigger, stronger, or in charge. And it takes real wisdom to know the difference between speaking up because something is genuinely wrong and complaining because you did not get what you wanted.

This packet is about learning when and how to stand up for what is right — in your family, in your community, and eventually in the wider world. You are going to learn that helping your community is not just a nice thing to do — it is something you owe. You are going to learn the difference between tattling and standing up. And you are going to hear stories of people who spoke up when something was terribly wrong, even when it cost them dearly.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **The habit of speaking up when something is genuinely wrong** — not tattling, not complaining, but standing up
2. **The distinction between tattling and standing up** — reporting to get someone in trouble versus reporting because something genuinely wrong is happening
3. **The idea that helping the community is a real obligation** — not optional charity but something you owe
4. **The ability to disagree respectfully** — expressing disagreement without disrespect
5. **Stories of courage** — real people who spoke up at great cost, appropriate for young learners
6. **The beginning of moral responsibility** — the idea that knowing something is wrong and staying silent makes you part of the problem
content.core_content:
### Part 1: You Are Part of Something Bigger

You are not alone in the world. You live in a family. You live in a neighborhood. You live in a community. You might go to a school, a church, a sports team, or a club. In all of these places, you are part of a group — and being part of a group means something.

It means you have responsibilities. Not just to yourself, but to the other people in your group. If you are on a team and you do not show up for practice, the team suffers. If you are in a family and you refuse to help with chores, the family suffers. If you are in a community and you never help anyone else, the community is weaker because you are in it instead of stronger.

**Helping your community is not just a nice thing to do. It is something you owe.** The community helps you — it provides safety, friendship, shared resources, and a place to belong. In return, you contribute. You help. You show up. You do your part.

This is the first and simplest form of citizenship: being a person who makes the group stronger by being in it.

### Part 2: The Difference Between Tattling and Standing Up

There is an important difference that many children (and many adults) get confused about.

**Tattling** is when you report something to get someone in trouble. The goal is not to fix a problem — it is to cause consequences for someone else. "Johnny is chewing gum!" when gum-chewing does not hurt anyone is tattling.

**Standing up** is when you report something because something genuinely wrong is happening and someone needs to know. "Sarah is hitting the little kids on the playground" is standing up — because someone is being hurt, and the person being hurt may not be able to stop it themselves.

The test is simple: **Is someone being harmed? Is something genuinely unfair or dangerous?** If yes, speaking up is standing up, and it is the right thing to do. If no — if you are just annoyed, or if you want someone to get in trouble — that is tattling, and it is not the same thing.

This distinction matters because the world needs people who stand up. It does not need people who tattle. And if you cannot tell the difference when you are young, you will struggle to tell the difference when you are older and the stakes are much higher.

### Part 3: Disagreeing Without Being Disrespectful

Sometimes you will think a rule is wrong. Sometimes you will think an adult is making a mistake. Sometimes you will disagree with someone who is in charge.

Here is what most people never learn: **you can disagree with someone in authority and still be respectful.** Disagreement and disrespect are not the same thing.

Respectful disagreement sounds like: "I understand the rule, but I think it is unfair because..." or "Can I explain why I see it differently?" or "I do not think this is right, and here is why."

Disrespectful disagreement sounds like: "That is stupid!" or refusing to listen, or yelling, or stomping away.

The person who can disagree respectfully is far more powerful than the person who throws a tantrum. Adults who can be told the truth — who listen when someone disagrees with them — are stronger, not weaker. And the child who learns to speak truth respectfully is practicing a skill that will serve them for their entire life.

### Part 4: Ruby Bridges — A Six-Year-Old Who Changed History

In 1960, a six-year-old girl named Ruby Bridges became the first Black child to attend an all-white school in New Orleans, Louisiana. She was the same age as some of you.

Every morning, Ruby walked into William Frantz Elementary School past crowds of screaming adults who did not want her there. They shouted terrible things. They threatened her. Some parents pulled their children out of the school so they would not have to be in the same building with her. For months, Ruby sat in a classroom with only her teacher, Mrs. Henry, because the other children's parents would not let them attend.

Ruby did not fight back. She did not scream or cry in front of the crowds. She walked in every day, did her schoolwork, and went home. When asked about the people who shouted at her, she said she prayed for them.

Ruby Bridges was not trying to make a political statement. She was a child going to school. But her courage — and the courage of her parents, who sent her knowing what she would face — helped change the entire country. Within a few years, schools across the South began to integrate.

**The lesson:** Courage is not the absence of fear. Ruby was afraid. Courage is doing the right thing even when you are afraid. And sometimes, the person who stands up is not a powerful adult with an army — it is a six-year-old girl with a book bag.

### Part 5: Martin Luther King Jr. — Speaking Up With Love

Martin Luther King Jr. believed that when something is deeply wrong, you have a duty to stand up against it — but you must stand up with love, not with hatred.

King lived in a country where Black people were treated as less than human by the law itself. They could not eat at the same restaurants, drink from the same water fountains, or go to the same schools as white people. These were not just customs — they were laws, enforced by police and courts.

King did not try to change these laws with violence. He organized marches. He gave speeches. He led boycotts — refusing to use buses that treated Black riders unfairly. He went to jail many times. And every time, he refused to hate the people who were treating him and his community unjustly.

King said: "Darkness cannot drive out darkness; only light can do that. Hate cannot drive out hate; only love can do that."

He believed that when you stand up against injustice, you must do it in a way that shows the injustice for what it is — not by becoming unjust yourself. This is incredibly hard. It takes more strength to respond to hatred with dignity than to respond with hatred of your own.

### Part 6: When Something Is Wrong and You Stay Silent

Here is something uncomfortable but important: **when you see something wrong happening and you say nothing, you are part of the problem.**

Imagine a playground where a bully is picking on a smaller child. There are ten other children watching. None of them help. None of them tell an adult. They just watch, or walk away.

The bully is doing the wrong thing. But the ten children who watched and did nothing also made a choice. They chose silence. They chose comfort. They chose to protect themselves instead of protecting someone who needed help.

This does not make them as bad as the bully. But it does mean they are not innocent. Silence in the face of something wrong is a choice — and it is a choice with consequences.

You will face moments like this in your life. Everyone does. The question is not whether you will be afraid — you will be. The question is whether you will let your fear stop you from doing what you know is right.

### Part 7: Helping Is Not Optional

Some people think of helping the community as charity — a nice thing you do if you feel like it. This packet asks you to think about it differently.

If you live in a community — and everyone does — then you benefit from that community every day. You benefit from the roads, the safety, the shared resources, the fact that other people follow rules and treat each other decently. These benefits are not free. They exist because other people contribute.

When you help — when you pick up trash that is not yours, when you stand up for someone being treated unfairly, when you volunteer, when you do your share of the work — you are paying back some of what you have received. This is not charity. This is responsibility.

The person who takes from the community and never gives back is not independent or clever. They are a free rider — someone who benefits from others' contributions without making their own. The sovereign person — the person this curriculum is building you to become — carries their share and more.

### Part 8: The Courage to Be Honest

There is one more kind of standing up that is especially hard: being honest when it would be easier to lie, when the truth will get you in trouble, or when everyone around you is going along with something you know is wrong.

Sometimes everyone in a group agrees on something that is not true — because it is comfortable, because the person who disagrees will be unpopular, because it is easier to go along. The person who says "I do not think that is right" in a room full of people who disagree takes a real risk. They might be laughed at. They might be excluded. They might lose friends.

But the person who stays honest when it costs something is building something that the person who goes along will never have: **integrity.** Integrity means your words and your beliefs and your actions all match. It means you can be trusted — by others, and by yourself.

This is the deepest form of moral responsibility: not just helping when it is convenient, not just standing up when it is easy, but being honest when it is hard.
content.formative_practices:
### Study 1: The Read-Through [Ongoing]
Read the stories of Ruby Bridges, Martin Luther King Jr., and other figures who stood up for what was right. After each reading, the student narrates the story and discusses: what was wrong? What did this person do? What did it cost them? Why did they do it anyway?

### Study 2: Tattling vs. Standing Up Exercise [Early in the Packet]
Present the student with scenarios — some that are tattling situations and some that are standing-up situations. The student sorts them and explains their reasoning. Discuss the test: Is someone being harmed? Is something genuinely wrong?

### Study 3: Ruby Bridges Study [Mid-Packet]
Read *The Story of Ruby Bridges* or another age-appropriate account. Study photographs from the period. Discuss: how old was Ruby? What did she face every day? Where did her courage come from? What would you have done?

### Study 4: King's Words [Mid-Packet]
Read selections from *Martin's Big Words* or another age-appropriate account of King's life. Focus on his actual words. Discuss: what did King mean by fighting injustice with love? Why did he refuse to use violence? Was his approach effective?

### Study 5: The Bystander Discussion [Mid-to-Late Packet]
Read *Each Kindness* or tell the story of a situation where someone did not stand up. Discuss: what did it feel like to stay silent? Is there a difference between doing something wrong and watching something wrong? What would you want someone to do if you were the one being treated unfairly?

### Study 6: Community Contribution Project [Late Packet]
The student identifies a specific way to contribute to their community — volunteering, helping a neighbor, organizing something for their group. They plan and execute the contribution. Afterward, they narrate what they did, what they learned, and how it felt.

### Study 7: The Courage Journal [End of Packet]
The student keeps a brief journal for two weeks, noting moments when they (or someone they observed) had the opportunity to stand up, speak out, or help. They note what happened — whether the person acted or stayed silent, and what the result was. At the end, they narrate what they observed and what they learned about courage in everyday life.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Tattling vs. Standing Up
**Developing well:** The student can clearly distinguish the two and explain the test. They apply this distinction in their own life — choosing not to tattle and choosing to stand up when appropriate.
**Developing slowly:** The student still confuses the two or defaults to one pattern (always reporting everything, or never reporting anything).

### 2. Stories of Courage
**Developing well:** The student can narrate Ruby Bridges' and King's stories with specific detail and personal reflection. They show emotional engagement — these stories are not just facts but lived experiences they have imaginatively entered.
**Developing slowly:** Narrations are factual but flat. No emotional connection. No personal reflection.

### 3. Community Responsibility
**Developing well:** The student understands that helping the community is owed, not optional. They have completed a community contribution and can articulate what they learned.
**Developing slowly:** The student still treats helping as optional charity or resists the idea that they owe anything to the community.

### 4. Respectful Disagreement
**Developing well:** The student can express disagreement with authority figures respectfully and does so when they believe something is genuinely wrong. They can explain why respectful disagreement is more effective than disrespectful disagreement.
**Developing slowly:** The student either never disagrees (compliance without conviction) or disagrees disrespectfully (defiance without principle).

### 5. Moral Courage in Practice
**Developing well:** The student has demonstrated moral courage in their own life — speaking up when something was wrong, being honest when it was hard, helping when it would have been easier not to. The courage journal shows specific instances.
**Developing slowly:** The student can discuss courage in stories but has not yet practiced it in their own life.
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Punishing Honest Disagreement
If the student disagrees with you and you respond with punishment or dismissal, you have undermined the entire packet. You are teaching them that authority figures cannot be told the truth. This does not mean you must accept every objection — it means you must receive every objection with respect before responding.

### Making Courage Sound Easy
Ruby Bridges was terrified. King was exhausted, jailed, and eventually murdered. If you present moral courage as easy — as something that naturally follows from being a good person — you are setting the student up for failure when they discover it is hard. Be honest: courage costs something. That is what makes it courage.

### Skipping the Community Contribution
The community contribution project is not optional enrichment. It is the practical application of the packet's core idea. A student who can talk about civic responsibility but has never practiced it has learned a concept, not developed a capacity.

### Moralizing Instead of Storytelling
At Stretching Branches, moral lessons are absorbed through stories, not lectures. If your sessions consist of explaining to the student what they should believe about courage and responsibility, you have lost the method. Read the stories. Let the stories do the work. The student's narration tells you what they absorbed.
mentor_guide.narration_evaluation:
### Prompt 1: Ruby Bridges
**Strong:** Specific details — her age, the school, the crowds, Mrs. Henry. Personal reflection — "I think she was brave because..." or "I would be scared if..." Emotional engagement with the story.
**Weak:** "Ruby Bridges went to a school." No detail, no engagement.

### Prompt 2: Tattling vs. Standing Up
**Strong:** Clear distinction with own examples. Can explain the harm test. Shows internalization, not just memorization.
**Weak:** Confused or cannot give examples beyond what was discussed.

### Prompt 3: King's Approach
**Strong:** Can explain nonviolence in their own words. Shows understanding that refusing to use hatred requires more strength than using it. May raise their own questions.
**Weak:** "He was nice to people." No depth of understanding.

### Prompt 4: Staying Silent
**Strong:** Honest self-reflection. Can articulate what it feels like to stay silent and why silence matters. May share a personal experience.
**Weak:** Denial — "I always speak up." No genuine self-examination.

### Prompt 5: Community Responsibility
**Strong:** Can explain the reciprocal relationship — the community gives to you, you give to the community. Uses own experience from the contribution project.
**Weak:** "You should help people because it is nice."

### Prompt 6: Being Honest
**Strong:** Shares a specific personal experience. Describes the difficulty honestly. Shows growth.
**Weak:** Generic or avoidant. No personal engagement.
